roof_system: Predominantly membrane roof with a separate darker low-slope field; primary field appears to be coated membrane or thermoplastic membrane, with an adjacent darker membrane section of uncertain type.

possible_roof_systems.evidence: Large light-colored low-slope roof field appears smooth and uniform from aerial view, consistent with a reflective thermoplastic membrane or coated membrane.
The darker central roof field is flat and membrane-like; the dark color and low reflectivity could fit EPDM, though aerial evidence is insufficient to confirm.
The light tan roof surfaces look uniformly weathered and may represent an aged membrane with coating, based on the consistent color field and low-slope commercial profile.

roof_age_estimate: Likely original-era or older low-slope roof components consistent with a 1969 industrial building, though aerial imagery cannot confirm install date.

notes: Visible darkened roof field contrasts strongly with the adjacent lighter roof areas, suggesting a distinct membrane section or possible coating/weathering pattern.
Multiple rooftop penetrations and equipment are visible, increasing flashing and seal maintenance risk on a low-slope roof.
No clear widespread ponding is visible from this aerial angle, but low-slope drainage performance cannot be fully verified from overhead imagery alone.

observations: Industrial warehouse building with multiple low-slope roof sections.
One large central dark roof field is visibly distinct from the surrounding lighter roof surfaces.
Several vents, curbs, and rooftop units/penetrations are present on the roof.
Roof surfaces appear generally serviceable from aerial view, but the tonal contrast suggests maintenance or aging differences between sections.
No obvious tree overhang or major debris accumulation is visible at the roof edges.
summary: Aerial imagery dated 03/11/2018 - 03/17/2018 shows a low-slope industrial roof with multiple membrane fields, including a large darker section and lighter weathered sections that may indicate different roof materials, coating, or age-related discoloration. Visible penetrations and rooftop equipment increase flashing and leak potential. No clear ponding or heavy debris is evident, but the roof presents moderate serviceability risk and the older imagery may understate more
recommendation: Have a qualified commercial roof-coating contractor perform an onsite inspection and moisture testing where warranted, confirm the actual roof system and warranty eligibility, identify required repairs and surface preparation, and provide a reliable project-price proposal. If the roof is a suitable low-slope membrane in restorable condition, evaluate a silicone roof restoration option before overlay or replacement; when properly designed and installed, a qualifying silicone system may reduce tear-off waste, cost, and disruption, create a seamless monolithic white reflective cool-roof surface, withstand ponding water, improve UV and hail resistance, and qualify for a manufacturer warranty of up to 20 years. If onsite findings show widespread moisture, failed insulation, deck or structural deterioration, incompatible substrate, or other non-restorable conditions, evaluate overlay or full replacement instead.
